text: This operation is a robotic assisted splenectomy for a symptomatic, nineteen centimeter splenic cyst. Prior to the operation, this patient received the Haemophilus influenzae B, the pneumococcus, and the meningococcus vaccination.
The patient is positioned in a lazy right lateral decubitus position, lying on their right side with a slight tilt to facilitate surgical access.

The surgeon begins the operation by mobilizing the splenic flexure, carefully transecting the splenocolic ligament to prevent tension on the spleen and avoid splenic capsular avulsion, which could lead to bleeding.
The surgeon enters the lesser sac and uses a vessel sealer to perform a counterclockwise dissection, transecting the gastrosplenic ligament.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to safely transect the short gastric vessels originating from the splenic artery. Following the mobilization of the greater curve of the stomach, the splenic hilum is exposed, which has been shifted to a more anterior location due to the expanding splenic cyst.

The surgeon releases the lateral splenophrenic ligament, exposing the posterior aspect of the spleen. This maneuver provides access to both the anterior and posterior splenic hilum, preparing for potential clamping of the pedicle in case of bleeding.
The surgeon observes the bulky spleen caused by the splenic cyst, which limits retraction. Attention is shifted to the dissection of the inferior splenic hilum.

The surgeon uses a white load vascular stapler to transect a portion of the splenic hilum, with the splenic artery visible traveling along the superior aspect of the pancreas and branching into upper and lower polar arteries.

The surgeon releases the stapler and prepares to control potential bleeding with a grasper, while the assistant holds a proline suture for possible ligation of larger vessels. The splenic artery is visible, branching near the hilum or extending five to ten centimeters away.

The surgeon transects the radial branches of the splenic artery individually using a vessel sealer, while carefully skeletonizing the artery to avoid injury to the nearby pancreatic tail. Lighter attachments are also transected with the vessel sealer.
The surgeon performs a piecemeal dissection, progressively revealing and isolating additional arterial branches leading to the splenic hilum.

The surgeon returns to the inferior aspect of the hilum, revealing the posterior border of the spleen. The final pedicle of the hilum is visible, with concern that a portion of the pancreatic tail may be within the tissue. The surgeon uses a stapler with a lateral twist in articulation to clear a segment of denser tissue.

The surgeon releases the lateral and superior attachments of the spleen after transecting the hilum. Due to inflammation caused by an expanding cyst, the spleen remains cumbersome. The surgeon drains the cyst to facilitate access to the remaining splenic ligaments.
The surgeon drains two liters of fluid from the cyst, fully evacuating it to facilitate better access to the remaining splenic attachments.
The surgeon encounters dense inflammatory adhesions between the diaphragm and surrounding tissues, carefully navigating these areas to avoid causing a pneumothorax while using electrocautery.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection and a vessel sealer to carefully separate tissue, minimizing the risk of injury to the diaphragm. The diaphragm is visible as the surgeon navigates around it, ensuring safe dissection.

The surgeon marks the abdominal midline with a pin while the patient is in the supine position. The patient is then placed in the lateral decubitus position, and a three-centimeter extraction incision is made in the supraumbilical location, with the marked midline and linea alba clearly visible.

The surgeon removes the spleen using morselization with ring forceps and finger fracture technique. A 45mm and 60mm stapler are available for stapling the splenic hilum, with the smaller stapler potentially useful for sequential transections.

The surgeon irrigates the splenic bed and evaluates it for bleeding after the spleen is freed from attachments. A 15mm endocatch bag is introduced through the extended 3cm incision in the abdominal midline for spleen morcellation and removal.
Leaving a surgical drain is debatable but recommended if continued bleeding, seroma, or pancreatic leak are of concern. Thank you for watching.
